title: Ensure that the audit policy covers key security concerns

input_row.title: Ensure that the audit policy covers key security concerns
input_row.description: Ensure that the audit policy created for the cluster covers key security concerns.
input_row.rationale: Security audit logs should cover access and modification of key resources in the cluster, to enable them to form an effective part of a security environment. Impact: Increasing audit logging will consume resources on the nodes or other log destination.
input_row.audit: Review the audit policy provided for the cluster and ensure that it covers at least the following areas :- • Access to Secrets managed by the cluster. Care should be taken to only log Metadata for requests to Secrets, ConfigMaps, and TokenReviews, in order to avoid the risk of logging sensitive data. • Modification of pod and deployment objects. • Use of pods/exec, pods/portforward, pods/proxy and services/proxy. For most requests, minimally logging at the Metadata level is recommended (the most basic level of logging).
input_row.remediation: Consider modification of the audit policy in use on the cluster to include these items, at a minimum. Default Value: By default Kubernetes clusters do not log audit information. References: 1. https://github.com/k8scop/k8s-security- dashboard/blob/master/configs/kubernetes/adv-audit.yaml 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug-application-cluster/audit/#audit-policy 3. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/cluster/gce/gci/configure- helper.sh#L735
input_row.impact: Increasing audit logging will consume resources on the nodes or other log destination.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:**
This control requires a subjective review of the audit policy file content to ensure it covers specific key security concerns (e.g., access to Secrets, modification of pods/deployments, use of exec/portforward/proxy). The Kubernetes API does not expose the contents of the audit policy file; it only references the file path via the API server's --audit-policy-file flag. The actual rules and granularity (e.g., logging level for specific resources/actions) must be visually inspected in the policy file itself, which is typically stored on the master node's filesystem and not accessible via kubectl or the Kubernetes API.

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. Access the Kubernetes master node(s) via SSH or console with sufficient privileges.
2. Locate the audit policy file referenced by the API server’s --audit-policy-file flag (commonly found in the API server manifest, e.g., /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml).
3. Open and review the audit policy file.
4. Verify that the policy includes rules covering at least:
   - Access to Secrets, ConfigMaps, and TokenReviews (ensure only metadata is logged for these to avoid sensitive data exposure).
   - Modification events for pod and deployment objects.
   - Use of pods/exec, pods/portforward, pods/proxy, and services/proxy.
   - Logging at least at the Metadata level for most requests.
5. Confirm that the policy aligns with the CIS recommendations and does not log sensitive data unnecessarily.
6. Document findings and remediate the policy if gaps are identified.

**SOURCE:** CIS KUBERNETES BENCHMARK V1.11.1 PDF
